Alright, you're back here. Let's keep rolling here with number three. We must have what I call stopping off points in our scripts. In other words, you don't just keep talk, talk, talk, talk, talking all the way through your script. You want to explain an idea, put a thought out there that has meaning, has power, and then you say, make sense, you follow me so far. Give your prospect a chance to check in with you or else you become a talking head and you break rapport and the prospect tunes out and you can't close. So you have to have a little stopping off points along the way with make sense, you follow me so far. Okay, great. Now, here's an important point. If you say to the prospect, does that make sense? And they say, well, not really. You don't just ask through it, you keep going. You have to say, okay, you slow down, you back up, re-explain your points. Now, does it make sense? They'll say, yes, now it does. Because if you do not address the client's objection or just a question right there, their uncertainty, you know what they say? This person's not listening to me. They just want to make a sale. They don't hear me. You break rapport and you can't influence anybody. So again, we have these stopping off points and just, you know, in the real world, it's very, very rare that when you say to someone, you're following me so far, they'll say, no, they'll say, yeah, I gotcha. It just keeps you in rapport and also gets your prospect in the habit of saying yes. It creates that consistency which helps you then close the deal on the backside. Okay, let's move on. Number four, you must write your scripts in the spoken word, not dramatically perfect English. This is the chief mistake I see people make with all non straight line scripts. And what happens is this, if you construct a script and it's written in grammatically perfect English, no one could use it. It's unusable. A script has to reflect the spoken word and be in not slang, but in common language that people would normally say or else it just sounds like you're reading from a script. You get it? Now that must be balanced with the fact that you also have to sound like an expert. So what you don't want to do is simply just be all, hey, bro, it's great. That's not what I mean. What I mean is you want to drop certain words and a perfect example would be in the movie The Wolf of Wall Street when I wrote that script for Leo, where Leo is making his first pitch. In fact, I'll show you to you right here. Let's play it really quick. Name of the company, AeroTine International. It is a cutting edge high tech firm out of the Midwest. All right. Now notice in that script, when he says name of the company, AeroTine International, it's a cutting edge high tech firm out of the Midwest. Notice he doesn't say the name of the company is AeroTine International. He says name of the company, AeroTine International. That's how you would speak in non-grammatically correct English, but it sounds great. So he drops the word the and is. Now why did he do that? Because I wrote this script and in the script, those words were not there. I said, Leo, if you want to sound great, here's how you speak. He said, great, makes perfect sense. So watch, name of the company, AeroTine International. It sounds awesome. So again, you cannot write your scripts in that perfect, grammatical English. Read it back to yourself again until it sounds perfect. Okay. Now let's move on to number five. It must flow perfectly. Well, I say flow like butter. This goes back to the same thing about, you know, name of the company, AeroTine International. So when a script is written perfectly, it flows perfectly. To that end, I want you to construct your script and then as you polish it and read through it, you want to do role playing and you want to take out any words or phrases that are tongue twisters that are difficult to say that trip people up. So you remember the idea with a great script is that it's not just for a natural born closer or a great speaker. It's even the lowest person on the tone of pole, the absolute beginner, the novice who really doesn't have that much natural sales. They can be taught to read these sets of words perfectly because all the tongue twisters have been removed. It has the exact right number of beats and syllables in each sentence and it just makes perfect sense. And by doing that, you create this essentially this blueprint that anyone can follow from start to finish. So that's why that rule is so crucial. Next, number six, strips must be honest and ethical. You're probably laughing because it's coming from me. Well, here's the deal. Just so you know, the scripts I wrote back in, they actually were, they were honest, but I wouldn't say I'm quite ethical. And that was a mistake. All right. And those I didn't lie in the scripts, but sometimes I omitted material facts, which is incorrect. Do not do that. Let me explain why. Number one, it's wrong. You don't want to lie. You want to be an ethical, just a terrible way of going through life. Trust me, I'm a million times happier now. So whatever you do, please be honest and ethical, not just with your stripping all aspects of using the straight line. And you know, I feel that way, right? Let me take a different tact though. What happens is if you're a manager, sales manager, owner of a company and you allow a script to be used, that's not quite ethical. You know what you're telegraphing to your entire sales force, guys, it's open field running. Go just go rape and pillage the village. Others, your company, your business, and even you as an individual sales person, you always become the sort of this magnification of what you start at. See, over time things have become almost a character of themselves. So if it starts off with a hint of dishonesty, go down the road, it's like wacky. It gets crazy. What you want to do is start off with absolute honesty, absolute ethics, absolute integrity, and maintain that the whole way through. That doesn't mean you close less sales, means you close more sales and eventually bigger sales and get massive repeat business. Now, before I move on to number seven here, I want to take a short break here and give you a quiz because the next one's a bear. Okay, so let's stop right now. Go take your quiz and when you come back, we'll dig into number seven, which is going to be one that's going to blow you away. I'll see you soon.
